{{Short description|Japanese mythological figure and god}}

{{Infobox deity
| type = 
| father = [[Amenohoakari]]
| mother = {{ill|Amemichihime|ja|天道日女命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemichihime|1=Amato Hime-no-Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q121355874}}
| children = {{ill|Ame no Murakumo|ja|大沼主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Murakumo|qq=}}
| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}
| parents = {{ill|Amanosakitama no Mikoto|WD=Q106241395}}
| offspring = {{ill|Ame no Murakumo|WD=Q106241464}}
}}

'''Takakuraji''' is a Japanese mythological figure. He helped with [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]]. [[Takemikazuchi]] placed his sword {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsu-no-mitama|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|布都御魂}} in Takakuraji's warehouse. Takakuraji found it and gave it to Jimmu, after being told to do such in a dream.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/146 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/146 |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Tb3OEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA89 |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003-11-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-57607-468-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-05-23 |title=Futsunomitama {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |access-date=2023-12-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230523140733/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |archive-date=2023-05-23 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ay0hEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set C: Language and Literature (8 vols) |date=2021-03-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90101-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Olson |first=Carl |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=GETPEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA554 |title=Sacred Texts Interpreted [2 volumes]: Religious Documents Explained [2 volumes] |date=2017-10-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-4408-4188-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qOvnhnN_VqsC&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A |date=2008-01-01 |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |isbn=978-1-60520-145-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=roEfAAAAYAAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |date=1896 |publisher=Society |isbn=978-0-524-05347-8 |language=en}}</ref>

The [[Kujiki]] says he is the son of [[Nigihayahi]].

He is also said to be the great grandson of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and ancestor of the [[Owari clan|Owari]] clans.<ref name="hoga-2006a">{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>

== Genealogy ==
The {{nihongo|Amabe clan genealogy|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}} is a famous document housed at Kono Shrine. It is from early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan. The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tanba and Tango. The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref>

In ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High [[:en:Heaven|Heaven]]) to [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba]] provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of [[Owari clan]], {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>

However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''Amabe clan genealogy'', which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the [[:en:List of water deities|sea deity]] [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, Takakuraji, as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref name="hoga-2006a" />

{{Generations of Jimmu}}

Takakuraji found it and gave it to Jimmu, after being told to do such in a dream.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/146 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/146 |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Tb3OEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA89 |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003-11-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-57607-468-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-05-23 |title=Futsunomitama {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |access-date=2023-12-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230523140733/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |archive-date=2023-05-23 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ay0hEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set C: Language and Literature (8 vols) |date=2021-03-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90101-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Olson |first=Carl |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=GETPEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA554 |title=Sacred Texts Interpreted [2 volumes]: Religious Documents Explained [2 volumes] |date=2017-10-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-4408-4188-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qOvnhnN_VqsC&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A |date=2008-01-01 |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |isbn=978-1-60520-145-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=roEfAAAAYAAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |date=1896 |publisher=Society |isbn=978-0-524-05347-8 |language=en}}</ref>

'''Takakuraji''' is a Japanese mythological figure. He helped with [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]]. [[Takemikazuchi]] placed his sword {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsu-no-mitama|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|布都御魂}} in Takakuraji's warehouse. Takakuraji found it and gave it to Jimmu, after being told to do such in a dream.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/146 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/146 |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Tb3OEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA89 |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003-11-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-57607-468-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-05-23 |title=Futsunomitama {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |access-date=2023-12-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230523140733/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |archive-date=2023-05-23 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ay0hEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set C: Language and Literature (8 vols) |date=2021-03-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90101-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Olson |first=Carl |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=GETPEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA554 |title=Sacred Texts Interpreted [2 volumes]: Religious Documents Explained [2 volumes] |date=2017-10-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-4408-4188-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qOvnhnN_VqsC&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A |date=2008-01-01 |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |isbn=978-1-60520-145-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=roEfAAAAYAAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |date=1896 |publisher=Society |isbn=978-0-524-05347-8 |language=en}}</ref>
The [[Kujiki]] says he is the son of [[Nigihayahi]].
He is also said to be the great grandson of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and ancestor of the [[Owari clan|Owari]] clans.<ref name="hoga-2006a">{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
== Genealogy == The {{nihongo|Amabe clan genealogy|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}} is a famous document housed at Kono Shrine. It is from early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan. The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tanba and Tango. The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref>
In ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High [[:en:Heaven|Heaven]]) to [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba]] provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of [[Owari clan]], {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''Amabe clan genealogy'', which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the [[:en:List of water deities|sea deity]] [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, Takakuraji, as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref name="hoga-2006a" />
{{Generations of Jimmu}}

'''Takakuraji''' is a Japanese mythological figure. He helped with [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]]. [[Takemikazuchi]] placed his sword {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsu-no-mitama|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|布都御魂}} in Takakuraji's warehouse. Takakuraji found it and gave it to Jimmu, after being told to do such in a dream.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/146 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/146 |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Tb3OEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA89 |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003-11-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-57607-468-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-05-23 |title=Futsunomitama {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |access-date=2023-12-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230523140733/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |archive-date=2023-05-23 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ay0hEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set C: Language and Literature (8 vols) |date=2021-03-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90101-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Olson |first=Carl |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=GETPEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA554 |title=Sacred Texts Interpreted [2 volumes]: Religious Documents Explained [2 volumes] |date=2017-10-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-4408-4188-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qOvnhnN_VqsC&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A |date=2008-01-01 |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |isbn=978-1-60520-145-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=roEfAAAAYAAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |date=1896 |publisher=Society |isbn=978-0-524-05347-8 |language=en}}</ref>
The [[Kujiki]] says he is the son of [[Nigihayahi]].
He is also said to be the great grandson of {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}} and ancestor of the [[Owari clan|Owari]] clans.<ref name="hoga-2006a">{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
== Genealogy == The {{nihongo|Amabe clan genealogy|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}} is a famous document housed at Kono Shrine. It is from early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan. The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango. The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref>
In ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|WD=Q4489450|lt=Heaven}}) to {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}} and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba}} provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of [[Owari clan]], {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''Amabe clan genealogy'', which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=sea deity}} {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, Takakuraji, as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref name="hoga-2006a" />
{{Generations of Jimmu}}

'''Takakuraji''' is a Japanese mythological figure. He helped with [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]]. [[Takemikazuchi]] placed his sword {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsu-no-mitama|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|布都御魂}} in Takakuraji's warehouse. Takakuraji found it and gave it to Jimmu, after being told to do such in a dream.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/146 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/146 |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Tb3OEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA89 |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003-11-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-57607-468-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-05-23 |title=Futsunomitama {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |access-date=2023-12-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230523140733/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |archive-date=2023-05-23 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ay0hEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set C: Language and Literature (8 vols) |date=2021-03-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90101-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Olson |first=Carl |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=GETPEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA554 |title=Sacred Texts Interpreted [2 volumes]: Religious Documents Explained [2 volumes] |date=2017-10-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-4408-4188-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qOvnhnN_VqsC&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A |date=2008-01-01 |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |isbn=978-1-60520-145-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=roEfAAAAYAAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |date=1896 |publisher=Society |isbn=978-0-524-05347-8 |language=en}}</ref>
The [[Kujiki]] says he is the son of [[Nigihayahi]].
He is also said to be the great grandson of {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}} and ancestor of the [[Owari clan|Owari]] clans.<ref name="hoga-2006a">{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
== Genealogy == The {{nihongo|Amabe clan genealogy|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}} is a famous document housed at Kono Shrine. It is from early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan. The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango. The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref>
In ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|WD=Q4489450|lt=Heaven}}) to {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}} and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba}} provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of [[Owari clan]], {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''Amabe clan genealogy'', which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=sea deity}} {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, Takakuraji, as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref name="hoga-2006a" />
{{Generations of Jimmu}}

'''Takakuraji''' is a Japanese mythological figure. He helped with [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]]. [[Takemikazuchi]] placed his sword {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsu-no-mitama|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|布都御魂}} in Takakuraji's warehouse. Takakuraji found it and gave it to Jimmu, after being told to do such in a dream.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/146 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/146 |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Tb3OEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA89 |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003-11-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-57607-468-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-05-23 |title=Futsunomitama {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |access-date=2023-12-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230523140733/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9414 |archive-date=2023-05-23 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ay0hEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set C: Language and Literature (8 vols) |date=2021-03-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90101-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Olson |first=Carl |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=GETPEAAAQBAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA554 |title=Sacred Texts Interpreted [2 volumes]: Religious Documents Explained [2 volumes] |date=2017-10-05 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing USA |isbn=978-1-4408-4188-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qOvnhnN_VqsC&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A |date=2008-01-01 |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |isbn=978-1-60520-145-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=roEfAAAAYAAJ&dq=Takakuraji&pg=PA115 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |date=1896 |publisher=Society |isbn=978-0-524-05347-8 |language=en}}</ref>
The [[Kujiki]] says he is the son of [[Nigihayahi]].
He is also said to be the great grandson of {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} and ancestor of the [[Owari clan|Owari]] clans.<ref name="hoga-2006a">{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
== Genealogy == The {{nihongo|Amabe clan genealogy|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}} is a famous document housed at Kono Shrine. It is from early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan. The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango. The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref>
In ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|WD=Q4489450|lt=Heaven|1=Heaven}}) to {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari|1=Owari Province}} and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba|1=Tanba Province}} provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of [[Owari clan]], {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''Amabe clan genealogy'', which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=sea deity|1=List of water deities}} {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, Takakuraji, as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref name="hoga-2006a" />
{{Generations of Jimmu}}
